coredata.dc:title: Bi2O3/Carbon quantum dots heterostructured photocatalysts with enhanced photocatalytic activity
coredata.dc:creator: Que, Qinghe
Xing, Yonglei
He, Zuoli
Yang, Yawei
Yin, Xingtian
Que, Wenxiu
coredata.dc:description: We reported a facile fabrication of Bi2O3/carbon quantum dots (CQDs) photocatalysts. All the synthesized samples were characterized and investigated. Firstly, irregular β-Bi2O3 nanosheets were obtained by a thermal treatment of Bi2O2CO3 precursor at 350°C for 3h in air. Then, different contents of CQDs were incorporated onto the as-obtained β-Bi2O3 nanosheets in ethanol solution by vigorous stirring. The experimental results demonstrated that the visible light absorption of β-Bi2O3 photocatalyst is greatly enhanced with the incorporation of CQDs. Furthermore, the fabricated heterostructure photocatalysts exhibited an enhanced photocatalytic activity over the photodegradation of Rhodamine B under the irradiation of visible light, owing to the separation of the photoinduced electrons and holes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon materials
Nanocomposites
Bi2O3/carbon quantum dots photocatalysts
Photodegradation
Rhodamine B

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of (a) CQDs, (c) Bi2O3 and (e) Bi2O3/CQDs composite, HRTEM image of (b) CQDs, (d) Bi2O3 and (f) Bi2O3/CQDs composite.
(a–d) Element mapping images, (e) XRD patterns and (f) C 1s spectra of the composite.
(a) UV–Vis diffuses reflectance spectra and (c) Photocatalytic activity of all the samples, (b) PL of the CQDs and (d) a scheme of the photocatalytic reaction mechanism.

head.ce:title: Bi2O3/Carbon quantum dots heterostructured photocatalysts with enhanced photocatalytic activity

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Electronic Materials Research Laboratory, International Center for Dielectric Research, Key Laboratory of the Ministry of Education, School of Electronic and Information Engineering, Xi’an Jiaotong University, Xi’an 710049, Shaanxi, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of the Ministry of Education
School of Electronic and Information Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We reported a facile fabrication of Bi2O3/carbon quantum dots (CQDs) photocatalysts. All the synthesized samples were characterized and investigated. Firstly, irregular β-Bi2O3 nanosheets were obtained by a thermal treatment of Bi2O2CO3 precursor at 350°C for 3h in air. Then, different contents of CQDs were incorporated onto the as-obtained β-Bi2O3 nanosheets in ethanol solution by vigorous stirring. The experimental results demonstrated that the visible light absorption of β-Bi2O3 photocatalyst is greatly enhanced with the incorporation of CQDs. Furthermore, the fabricated heterostructure photocatalysts exhibited an enhanced photocatalytic activity over the photodegradation of Rhodamine B under the irradiation of visible light, owing to the separation of the photoinduced electrons and holes.

ce:section.ce:para: To resolve the environmental pollution and energy crisis problems, oxide semiconductors, such as TiO2 [1], ZnO [2], and MnO2 [3], have been widely investigated as light-driven photocatalysts, which can only absorb ultraviolet light because of the large band gap. Up till now, great efforts have been made to enhance the absorption of visible light and separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs.

Most strikingly, bismuth oxide (Bi2O3), a p-type semiconductor with six polymorphic forms, has been applied in various fields, which have aroused researchers’ great attentions due to its high refractive index and thermal stability [4]. Although Bi2O3 has been known as a visible light driven photocatalyst for pollutant decomposition and water splitting, the quick recombination of photo-generated charge carriers leads to the less “active sites”, and largely hindered its photocatalytic performance [5,6]. Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a new carbon nanomaterial with low cost, low toxicity and high photostability, have been successfully incorporated into semiconductors, such as BiOCl, [7] Bi2WO6, [8] and Ag3PO4 [9]. Besides, CQDs can emit short wavelength light after absorbing long wavelength light, which is called up-conversion photoluminescence. Actually, to the best of our knowledge, the report on the preparation and characterization of the Bi2O3/CQDs composite was very scarce. Herein, a new Bi2O3/CQDs heterostructured photocatalyst was prepared, showing an enhanced photocatalytic activity under visible light. Moreover, the close contact between CQDs and Bi2O3 is advantageous to the transfer of electrons, and thus inhibiting the recombination of photogenerated carriers.

All the chemicals were used without any further purification. Typically, 2mmol Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was dissolved in 10ml 1M HNO3 solution. After stirred for 10min, 1.5mmol of citric acid was added into aforementioned solution. The pH value of the solution was adjusted to 4 with the aid of NaOH. Finally, a clear solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180°C for 24h. Thus, the white Bi2O2CO3 powders can be obtained by centrifugation, washed with distilled water and ethanol, and dried in a vacuum at 60°C for 6h. While the Bi2O3 powder was synthesized by the thermal treatment of the as-prepared Bi2O2CO3 precursor at 350°C for 3h in air [6]. CQDs were synthesized according to the method reported in Ref. [10]. A stoichiometric amount of L-Ascorbic acid was ultrasonically dissolved in 20ml of 50% ethanol in water, followed by a treatment at 160°C for 4h. After cooled down, the water phase solution was extracted with dichloromethane and dialyzed for 24h. The CQDs solution was thus obtained. Then, 0.1g Bi2O3 powders were ultrasonically dispersed in CQDs solution and continuously stirred for 2h. Finally the mixture was rinsed with distilled water and dried at 80°C for 12h. Base on the mass ratio of CQDs, the resultant Bi2O3/CQDs heterostructured photocatalysts were designated as Bi2O3/CQDs-1, Bi2O3/CQDs-2, Bi2O3/CQDs-4.

The photocatalytic performances of the samples were investigated by testing the Rhodamine B (RhB) degradation, using a 500W xenon lamp (λ≥400nm) as the light source. In a typical experiment, 40 mg of the samples and 40ml of RhB (5mgL−1) were added into a 100mL beaker. Before illumination, the solution was stirred for 30min in the dark to establish adsorption/desorption equilibrium. At certain time intervals, 4ml of solution was taken out and centrifuged for further analysis through a UV–vis spectrophotometer.

TEM and HRTEM images of the CQDs, Bi2O3 and Bi2O3/CQDs composites are shown in Fig. 1 . It can be seen from Fig. 1a and b that the CQDs are composed of many nanoparticles with the diameter of around 2–5nm, showing the interplanar spacing of 0.21nm corresponding to the (100) plane. In Fig. 1c and d, the Bi2O3 shows an irregular nanosheet-shaped structure and the lattice spacing of 0.34nm corresponding to the (201) crystal plane. Clearly, a large number of well-dispersed CQDs are evenly attached to the surface of the Bi2O3 nanosheets, as shown in Fig. 1e. Furthermore, the HRTEM of the CQDs within the red circles shown in Fig. 1f also demonstrates the formation of the Bi2O3/CQDs heterostructured material. Moreover, adjacent lattice fringes in the composite are in good consistent with the pure CQDs and Bi2O3.

As shown in Fig. 2 , it is clear to see the formation of Bi2O3/CQDs heterostructured materials. Furthermore, It can be seen from Fig. 2e for the β-Bi2O3 that the distinctive peaks at 2θ=28.2, 30.5, 32.0, 32.9, 46.5, 47.2, 55.8 can be indexed to (201), (211), (002), (220), (222), (400), (213) planes of β-Bi2O3 (PDF#27-0050), respectively. For the Bi2O3/CQDs composites, no typical carbon peaks can be observed, due to the high dispersion and the small amount of the CQDs in the Bi2O3/CQDs composite.

In addition, XPS was also conducted to prove the existence carbon quantum dots. Here, the peak at 284.6eV, resulted from XPS instrument itself, was used as reference. Apparently, band energies at 285.4 and 288.5eV, which are assigned to the CO and CCOH, together with those of O 1s at 529.25 and 531.77eV as shown in Fig. S1(a), can show the presence of carbon-oxygen bonds in the composite [10]. Besides, Fig. S1(b) shows the spectrum of Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 at 158.78 and 164.1eV.

As shown in Fig. 3 (a), the absorbance of the pure Bi2O3 material shows onset at 550nm, while the CQDs shows onset at 420nm. Obviously, all the CQDs modified Bi2O3 materials displays the light absorption at between CQDs and Bi2O3. Furthermore, the band gap energies are presented in Table S1, ESI, indicating the effects of the CQDs on Bi2O3.

According to the reported results, the CQDs prepared by ultrasonic-hydrothermal method can not only show normal down-conversion photoluminescence but also perform excellent up-conversion properties [11,12]. Hence, lower-energy light (near-infrared or infrared) can be converted into higher-energy light (ultraviolet or visible) by means of the up-conversion process, which can be testified by photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the CODs as shown in Fig. 3(b).

The RhB dye was used to evaluate the photocatalytic performance of the synthesized samples under visible light (λ>400nm) irradiation. As shown in Fig. 3(c), about 58.3% of RhB molecules can be decomposed over the pure Bi2O3 after 60min. In comparison, the Bi2O3/CQDs-2 sample shows the best photoactivity for the RhB degradation, where 92.5% of RhB molecules can be degraded after 60min of illumination, while only 89.5% and 77.2% of RhB molecules are degraded by Bi2O3/CQDs-1and Bi2O3/CQDs-4 samples, respectively.

In conclusion, a facile approach has been used to prepare the CQDs/Bi2O3 composite photocatalyst. All the CQDs modified Bi2O3 composites display enhanced photocatalytic activity for the degradation of RhB molecules under visible light irradiation, which is attributed to the excellent up-conversion property of CQDs and the efficient separation of the photogenerated electrons and holes pairs. Consequently, the photoactivity of the CQDs/Bi2O3 composites can be greatly improved. Here, the enhanced photoactivity of the CQDs/Bi2O3 composites may provide a new and valid strategy in the design of high-performance photocatalysts under visible light irradiation.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Research Fund for the Doctoral Program of Higher Education of China under grant 20120201130004, and the Science and Technology Developing Project of Shaanxi Province (2015KW-001), The SEM and TEM works were done at International Center for Dielectric Research, Xi'an Jiaotong University, Xi'an, China. The authors also thank Ms Dai and Mr Ma for their help in using SEM and TEM.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2017.07.115.
